title: Quiz: Logging and Distributed Tracing
description: Test your understanding of logging and tracing practices for quantum applications

questions.question: Why is distributed tracing particularly important for quantum applications?

options.text: Quantum applications always run faster than classical ones
Quantum applications span multiple execution domains (preprocessing, quantum execution, post-processing) that need unified visibility
Distributed tracing is required by quantum hardware providers
It is only needed for debugging, not production

questions.explanation: Quantum applications involve multiple phases: classical preprocessing, transpilation, quantum execution (with queue times), and post-processing. Distributed tracing provides visibility across all these domains to understand end-to-end behavior and identify bottlenecks.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of structured JSON logging over plain text logging?

options.text: JSON files are smaller than text files
JSON logs can be queried, filtered, and analyzed programmatically by log aggregation systems
JSON logging is faster to write
Plain text logging is not supported in production environments

questions.explanation: Structured JSON logging produces machine-readable records that can be indexed and queried by log aggregation systems (Elasticsearch, Splunk). This enables powerful analysis, filtering by fields like job_id or fidelity, and automated alerting based on log content.

questions.question: What is a correlation ID in the context of quantum application logging?

options.text: The measurement correlation between entangled qubits
A unique identifier that tracks a request through all stages of the pipeline
The ID assigned by the quantum hardware provider
A metric for measuring log quality

questions.explanation: A correlation ID is a unique identifier assigned to a request at the start of processing and propagated through all stages (preprocessing, transpilation, quantum execution, post-processing). This enables linking all related logs and traces for a single operation.

questions.question: In OpenTelemetry, what is a 'span'?

options.text: A connection between two quantum qubits
A named, timed operation representing a unit of work in a trace
A type of quantum gate
A log message format

questions.explanation: A span represents a single operation within a trace. It has a name, start/end timestamps, attributes, and can have parent/child relationships with other spans to represent the call hierarchy.

questions.question: Which of the following should be included in a structured log entry for quantum circuit execution? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Circuit name and depth
Backend name and job ID
The developer's personal phone number
Correlation/trace ID

questions.explanation: Structured logs for quantum execution should include circuit metadata (name, depth), backend information (name, job ID), and tracing context (correlation ID). Personal information should never be included in logs.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a chain hash in an audit trail?

options.text: To compress the audit log
To detect if any entries have been modified or deleted (tamper detection)
To encrypt the audit data
To speed up searching through entries

questions.explanation: A chain hash links each entry to all previous entries. If any entry is modified or removed, the chain hash will not match when recalculated, providing tamper detection for audit integrity.

questions.question: When implementing tracing for a VQE optimization, what would be appropriate span granularity?

options.text: One span for the entire VQE run only
Separate spans for each phase: preprocessing, each iteration, transpilation, execution, and post-processing
One span per qubit in the circuit
Spans should not be used for VQE

questions.explanation: Proper tracing granularity for VQE includes a root span for the entire optimization, with child spans for preprocessing, each iteration (with nested spans for transpilation and execution), and post-processing. This provides visibility into where time is spent.

questions.question: What Python feature is commonly used to propagate correlation IDs through async code without explicit parameter passing?

options.text: Global variables
ContextVars (context variables)
Environment variables
Class inheritance

questions.explanation: Python's contextvars module provides ContextVar, which maintains context-local state that properly propagates through async code. This is the recommended way to track correlation IDs without threading them through every function call.
